title: Why This Is the Most Exciting Time to Be Human | Ken Ono, Axiom Math
summary: Ken Ono, founding mathematician at Axiom Math, argues that humans cannot compete with AI on knowledge alone and should instead focus on curiosity, human judgment, and discovery. He uses Srinivasa Ramanujan as a case study to illustrate how institutional systems can miss genius and proposes that true intelligence in the AI age lies in qualities machines cannot replicate.
writeup: Ken Ono, a number theorist and founding mathematician at Axiom Math, recounts the moment frontier LLMs, through Epoch AI's Frontier Math benchmarking program, left him unable to construct math problems they could not solve. Rather than chasing the question of how to stay ahead of AI, he reframes intelligence itself: LLMs are extraordinary librarians and knowledge is now cheap, but judgment, concept creation, and cross-disciplinary pattern recognition remain scarce and valuable. The talk weaves in Ono's personal history with the Indian autodidact mathematician Ramanujan as a living argument that non-institutional, unconventional minds hold precisely the kind of intelligence that machines do not replicate.

key_points: Ono worked on Epoch AI's Frontier Math program, which hired professional mathematicians worldwide to construct problems hard enough to stump state-of-the-art LLMs; he found he could no longer reliably do so, a result that shook his professional identity.

The correct frame is not 'how do I stay ahead of AI' but rather what kind of intelligence matters now: LLMs function as the most comprehensive librarians ever built, meaning factual knowledge has become cheap, while human judgment, verification, and synthesis have become more expensive.

Ono argues that genuine intelligence is the ability to create new concepts, transfer patterns across disciplines, and string ideas together in novel ways; these capacities are rarely what schools at any level actually teach or reward.

Ramanujan, a self-taught mystic who flunked out of college twice and filled three notebooks with formulas before being discovered, served as Ono's model that unconventional, non-credentialed genius exists outside institutional pipelines and needs active discovery.

The Spirit of Ramanujan program, which Ono ran to find undiscovered mathematical talent, produced at least one example (Karina Hong, later his boss) of a career that would not have existed without an explicit search beyond standard academic channels.

Ono's own path through high school dropout, indifferent undergraduate years at the University of Chicago, and a late pivot to Ramanujan-inspired thesis work on Galois representations illustrates that the trajectory connecting unconventional curiosity to significant mathematics is fragile and contingent on encountering the right model at the right moment.
